stimulus: Project-based learning is a teaching approach gaining attention in schools. Instead of relying on textbook lessons, teachers ask students to investigate real-world problems and create original products to share their findings. For example, in Marisol Ortega’s science class, students design and build small wind turbines and test them in a local park. Researchers claim that, in addition to deepening students’ problem-solving skills, project-based learning improves student outcomes by boosting attendance and class participation.
stem: Which finding, if true, would most strongly support the underlined claim?

answerOptions.content: Classes that use project-based learning report some of the highest attendance and participation rates in the school.
Students who already excel at problem-solving are usually the ones who enroll in project-based learning classes.
Teachers find that preparing project-based lessons is more time-consuming than preparing traditional lessons.
Some parents prefer project-based learning because it gives students more independence at home.

rationale:
Choice A is correct because the claim specifically links project-based learning to better attendance and participation, and Choice A directly reports those higher rates as evidence.

Choice B is incorrect because it suggests the students were already engaged, which would weaken (not strengthen) the claim that the method itself boosts participation. Choice C is incorrect because teacher workload doesn’t address student attendance or participation. Choice D is incorrect because parent preferences are unrelated to the claim about classroom outcomes.
